name: xocto
documentation:
# xocto - utilities for Python services

Proven utilities used on Python services at Kraken Technologies. This library
works with Python 3.9 and above.

CI status:

[![CircleCI](https://circleci.com/gh/octoenergy/xocto/tree/main.svg?style=svg)](https://circleci.com/gh/octoenergy/xocto/tree/main)

Docs status:

[![Documentation Status](https://readthedocs.org/projects/xocto/badge/?version=latest)](https://xocto.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest)

- PyPI detail page: <https://pypi.python.org/pypi/xocto>
- Documentation: <https://xocto.readthedocs.io/>

functions.name: xocto.tracing.set_tags
documentation: Set multiple tags on the current span.

functions.name: xocto.tracing.set_tag
documentation: Set a tag on the current span.

functions.name: xocto.tracing.set_global_tag
documentation:
Set a tag on the current root span.

These tags will be associated to the entire Datadog trace.

classes.name: xocto.types.generic.Comparable
documentation: A way of describing an object that can be compared to another using some basic operations.

functions.name: xocto.numbers.quantise
documentation:
Round a number to an arbitrary integer base. For example:
\>>> quantise(256, 5)
255

Note that numbers equal to half of the rounding amount will always
round down. So:
\>>> quantise(15, 30)
0

functions.name: xocto.numbers.truncate_decimal_places
documentation:
Truncate a float (i.e round towards zero) to a given number of decimal places.

NB: Takes a decimal but returns a float!

```pycon
>>> truncate_decimal_places(12.364, 1)
12.3
```

```pycon
>>> round_decimal_places(-12.364, 1)
-12.3 # -12.3 is bigger than -12.4
```

```pycon
>>> round_decimal_places(12.364, 0)
12.0 # rounding to 0 returns float with no decmial part
```

functions.name: xocto.numbers.round_decimal_places
documentation:
Round a decimal to a given number of decimal places using a given rounding method.

By default, we use half-up rounding so that parts from half way between the given rounding
precision will be rounded up towards the greater number.

This differs from the default rounding since Python 3.0 which is also used elsewhere in Kraken
(which use "banker's"/half-even rounding, which is considered by IEEE 754 to be the recommended
default for decimal).

```pycon
>>> round_decimal_places(12.35, 1)
12.4
```

```pycon
>>> round_decimal_places(-12.35, 1)
-12.3 #-12.3 is bigger than -12.4
```

functions.name: xocto.numbers.round_to_integer
documentation:
Round a decimal to the nearest integer, using a given rounding method.

By default, we use half-up rounding.

This differs from the default rounding since Python 3.0 which is also used elsewhere in Kraken
(which use "banker's"/half-even rounding, which is considered by IEEE 754 to be the recommended
default for decimal).

functions.name: xocto.numbers.clip_to_range
documentation:
Clip the value to the min and max values given.

Values to be compared must be of the same type.

Example usage:
    >>> clip_to_range(10, minval=20, maxval=25)
    20
    >>> clip_to_range(date(2020, 1, 4), minval=date(2020, 1, 1), maxval=date(2020, 1, 3))
    date(2020, 1, 3)
    >>> clip_to_range(1.5, minval=1.0, maxval=2.0)
    1.5

functions.name: xocto.numbers.remove_exponent
documentation:
Util function for removing the exponent and trailing zeroes of a decimal.

From https://docs.python.org/3/library/decimal.html#decimal-faq

functions.name: xocto.numbers.random_int
documentation:
Return a pseudo-random integer based on the provided `length`.

    >>> random_int(3)
    114

classes.name: xocto.types.django.AuthenticatedRequest
documentation:
This class represents HttpRequests that are guaranteed to have an auth user associated
with them, for use when hinting requests that are known to be authenticated.

classes.name: xocto.exceptions.SettlementPeriodError
documentation: Basic exception raised while converting tz-aware datetime.

modules.name: xocto.storage.s3_select
documentation:
This file contains helper functions necessary for using the s3 select functionality.

Note:
    - Many comments have been taken directly from the boto3 [docs]
(https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.select_object_content)

functions.name: xocto.storage.s3_select.get_serializers_for_csv_file
documentation:
Returns input and output serialization dictionaries that should be used to perform a select_object_content query.

Note: It is recommended to use JSONOutputSerializer over CSVOutputSerializer while using ScanRange queries
because the former prevents premature chunked data. In other words, because scan range queries loop over the file
using byte range, you can expect the JSON output format to at least return one single row in each iteration. With
CSV output format, there is a high chance of receiving output that has been chunked to a certain byte.

functions.name: xocto.storage.s3_select.get_serializers_for_parquet_file
documentation:
Returns input and output serialization dictionaries that should be used to perform a select_object_content query.

Parquet files do not support input serialization or scan ranges.

classes.name: xocto.storage.s3_select.FileHeaderInfo
documentation: A bucket of strings used by the `select_object_content` function to read a CSV file.

methods.name: xocto.storage.s3_select.BaseSerializer.to_dict
documentation:
A helper method to remove None values from the dict and ensures all enum fields use their values
instead of an enum instance.

classes.name: xocto.storage.s3_select.ScanRange
documentation:
Specifies the byte range of the object to get the records from.

- Provide a start and an end value to perform an inclusive search.
- Provide only the start value to process search after the given X records.
- Provide only the end value to search the last X records of the file.

functions.name: xocto.health.check_migrations
documentation: Check if there are any migrations that haven't been applied yet.

functions.name: xocto.health._num_unapplied_migrations
documentation: Return the number of unapplied migrations.

modules.name: xocto.storage.files
documentation: A module for file-related functions.

functions.name: xocto.storage.files.size
documentation: Return the size of the file in bytes.

functions.name: xocto.storage.files.hashfile
documentation:
Get contents of file as hash. Use like so:
\>>> hashfile(open(myfile, 'rb'))
... 40c33eec0a307e7726992d3786f11a8c30309489455c33cfc14b36e6fbf7347b

functions.name: xocto.storage.files.convert_xlsx_to_csv
documentation:
Convert .xlsx files to CSV format using `openpyxl` and return a handle to the output file.

Args:
    xlsx_filepath:     Path of .xls or .xlsx file to convert.
    csv_filepath:       Location of result file referenced by returned handler. A temporary
                        file will be created if this arg is not present.
    encoding:           Encoding scheme for csv file.
    errors:             How file object should handle encoding errors.
    quoting:            The type of quoting the output CSV file should have
    delimiter:          The delimiter the output CSV file should have

functions.name: xocto.storage.files.convert_xls_to_csv
documentation:
Convert .xls files to CSV format using `xlrd` and return a handle to the output file.

Args:
    xls_filepath:       Path of .xls file to convert.
    csv_filepath:       Location of result file referenced by returned handler. A temporary
                        file will be created if this arg is not present.
    encoding:           Encoding scheme for csv file.
    errors:             How file object should handle encoding errors.
    quoting:            The type of quoting the CSV file should have
    delimiter:          The delimiter the output CSV file should have

functions.name: xocto.storage.files.remove_bom
documentation:
Remove the BOM mark from the beginning of a string if it exists.

This mark is sometimes added to excel files and causes issues when reading.

functions.name: xocto.storage.files.generate_csv_bytes
documentation:
Generates a csv file and transforms it to bytes.
reference: <https://okhlopkov.medium.com/dont-save-a-file-on-disk-to-send-it-with-telegram-bot-d7cd591fec2d>
\:param data: List containing the data rows
\:param headers: List containing the headers of the file

functions.name: xocto.storage.files.convert_csv_to_parquet
documentation:
Converts bytes from CSV format to Parquet.

```
param data

bytes contents for/from a CSV file

param na_values

Additional strings to recognize as NA/NaN. If dict passed, specific
```

per-column NA values.
\:param data_type: Data type for data or columns. You can Use str, object or a dict with
the column names and types. E.g. {‘a’: np.float64, ‘b’: np.int32, ‘c’: ‘Int64’}
\:return: bytes for a Parquet file

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods._to_timezone
documentation: Converts an aware datetime to another time zone.

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods._get_first_delivery_time
documentation: Return an aware datetime for the start of the first settlement period on a given date.

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods._get_delivery_date
documentation: Return the date of the settlement period relative to a tz-aware datetime.

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods.convert_sp_and_date_to_local
documentation: Return an aware datetime for the start of a given settlement period.

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods.convert_sp_and_date_to_utc
documentation: Return an UTC-aware datetime for the start of a given settlement period.

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods.convert_local_to_sp_and_date
documentation: Return the date and settlement period from a given tz-aware datetime.

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods.convert_utc_to_sp_and_date
documentation: Return the local date and settlement period from a given UTC-aware datetime.

functions.name: xocto.settlement_periods.number_of_periods_in_timedelta
documentation: Return the number of half-hourly settlement periods in the given timedelta

classes.name: xocto.events.utils.Timer
documentation: Context manager to allow easy timing of events.

functions.name: xocto.events.core.publish
documentation:
Publish an event.

- `params` are values that were used to create the event (eg the path of a
  request)
- `meta` are contextual values around the event (eg the IP address of the
  person making the request)

Note, structlog will add a timestamp.

functions.name: xocto.events.core._log
documentation: Log the event.

functions.name: xocto.events.core._request_meta
documentation: Extract relevant meta information from a request instance.

modules.name: xocto.fields.postgres.ranges
documentation: Adapter fields for postgres range types that return xocto.Range python objects.

classes.name: xocto.fields.postgres.ranges.FiniteDateRangeField

methods.name: xocto.fields.postgres.ranges.FiniteDateRangeField._upper_to_inclusive
documentation:
Transform an exclusive upper bound to an inclusive bound.

DateRanges (as all discrete ranges) are always stored as [) in the database, even if the
input was []. [X, Y] is equivalent to [X, Y+1) in the database. When we read the value
from the database, we need to subtract 1 day from the upper bound to return it to inclusive.

A DateRangeField with Inclusive-Inclusive [] bounds.

The underlying postgres type will always store the range as Inclusive-Exclusive [).
This field will always translate to [].

Accepts and returns xocto.ranges.FiniteDateRange objects.

classes.name: xocto.fields.postgres.ranges.FiniteDateTimeRangeField
documentation:
A DateTimeRangeField with Inclusive-Exclusive [) bounds that aren't infinite.

Accepts and returns xocto.ranges.FiniteDatetimeRange objects. Values are always
timezone-aware, and will be converted to the timezone specified in django settings.

classes.name: xocto.fields.postgres.ranges.HalfFiniteDateTimeRangeField
documentation:
A DateTimeRangeField with Inclusive-Exclusive [) bounds that allows an infinite/open upper bound.

Accepts and returns xocto.ranges.HalfFiniteDatetimeRange objects. Values are always
timezone-aware, and will be converted to the timezone specified in django settings.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.as_localtime
documentation:
Convert a tz aware datetime to localtime.

Wrapper for the `django.utils.timezone` function, taking the same arguments.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.as_utc
documentation: Wrapper for normalizing a datetime aware object into UTC.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.now
documentation: Return the current datetime in the local timezone.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.datetime
documentation: Return a datetime in the local timezone.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.date
documentation:
Return the date of the given datetime in the given timezone, defaulting to local time.

Note that `dt` must already be timezone-aware: it cannot be naive. It may be in UTC, for
example, or already in current local time, in which case the effect will be the same as
calling `.date()` on it. If `tz` is not provided, the function will return the date of `dt` in
the local time zone.
Returning dates

functions.name: xocto.localtime.today
documentation: Return the current date in the provided timezone (or the local timezone if none is supplied).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.yesterday
documentation: Return the previous date in the provided timezone (or the local timezone if none is supplied).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.tomorrow
documentation: Return the next date in the provided timezone (or the local timezone if none is supplied).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.days_in_the_past
documentation: Return n days before the current date (in the provided or local timezone).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.days_in_the_future
documentation: Return n days after the current date (in the provided or local timezone).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.months_in_the_past
documentation: Return n months before the current date (in the provided or local timezone).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.months_in_the_future
documentation: Return n months after the current date (in the provided or local timezone).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.date_of_day_before
documentation:
Return the date of the day before the datetime passed in.

That is, find the local date of the datetime passed in, subtract one day from it, and return it.

This is frequently useful when we are converting from the upper bound of a datetime range,
which by convention we treat as exclusive, to the upper bound of a date range, which by
convention we consider *inclusive*.

Note that `dt` must already be timezone-aware: it cannot be naive. It may be in UTC, for
example, or already in current local time, in which case the effect will be the same as
calling `.date()` on it and subtracting a day. If `tz` is not provided, the function will
return the date before `dt` in the local time zone.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.day_before
documentation: Return the date of the day before the passed date.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.day_after
documentation: Return the date of the day after the passed date.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.seconds_in_the_future
documentation: Return a datetime of the number of specifed seconds in the future.
Returning datetimes

functions.name: xocto.localtime.seconds_in_the_past
documentation: Return a datetime of the number of specifed seconds in the past.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.midnight
documentation:
Return a TZ-aware datetime for midnight of the passed date.

If date is None then the current date in the passed timezone is used (if no timezone is
supplied then we use the local timezone).
Converting dates into datetimes

functions.name: xocto.localtime.next_midnight
documentation:
Return the datetime for midnight of the following day to the date passed in.

This is intuitively what people think of as midnight.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.midday
documentation:
Return a TZ-aware datetime for midday of the passed date.

If date is None then the current date in the passed timezone is used (if no timezone is
supplied then we use the local timezone).

functions.name: xocto.localtime.datetime_from_date
documentation: Return a TZ-aware datetime for the hour of the passed date.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.datetime_from_epoch_timestamp
documentation: Return a TZ-aware datetime for the passed epoch timestamp.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.latest
documentation:
Return a TZ-aware datetime for the latest representable datetime of the passed date.

If date is None then the current date in the local timezone is used.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.combine
documentation:
Return a TZ-aware datetime obtained by combining the given date and time.

It's a TZ-aware wrapper around datetime.datetime.combine.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.date_boundaries
documentation:
Return a 2-tuple with the start and ending dt for the given date in the local timezone.

Note, be careful of using this with __range ORM queries as such queries are INCLUSIVE on the
boundaries.
Converting dates into datetime pairs

functions.name: xocto.localtime.month_boundaries
documentation:
Return the boundary datetimes of a given month.

The boundary consists of a 2-tuple of: midnight of the first day of the month and midnight of the first day of the next month.

For example:

    >>> month_boundaries(1, 2020)
    ((datetime.datetime(2020, 1, 1, 0, 0), datetime.datetime(2020, 2, 1, 0, 0))

functions.name: xocto.localtime.as_range
documentation:
Return a 2-tuple of the min and max datetimes for the given date.

This gives values that can be passed to the ORM __range filter.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.make_aware_assuming_local
documentation:
Just a wrapper for Django's method, which will takes a naive datetime, and makes it timezone
aware, assuming the current timezone if none is passed (which it isn't from this wrapper
function). It will also raise an exception if the passed datetime is already timezone-aware.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.make_aware_assuming_utc
documentation: Return a timezone-aware datetime (in UTC) given a naive datetime.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_utc
documentation: Test whether a given (timezone-aware) datetime is in UTC time or not.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_local_time
documentation: Test whether a given (timezone-aware) datetime is in local time or not.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.within_date_range
documentation: Check if two dates are within a range from each other.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.quantise
documentation:
'Round' a datetime to the nearest interval given by the `timedelta` argument.

For example:

    >>>> quantise(datetime.datetime(2020, 4, 1, 23, 21), timedelta(minutes=15))
    datetime.datetime(2020, 4, 1, 23, 30)

functions.name: xocto.localtime.nearest_half_hour
documentation: Return the nearest half-hour datetime to the passed datetime.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_last_day_of_month
documentation: Return True if the given date is the last day of the month.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.start_of_month
documentation: Return the start datetime of the month for dt passed - or of current month.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.end_of_month
documentation: Return the start datetime of the next month for dt passed - or of next month.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.first_day_of_month
documentation: Return the start date of the month for dt passed - or of current month.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.last_day_of_month
documentation: Return the last date of the month for dt passed - or of current month.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_n_days_until_end_of_month
documentation: Return whether today + n days is the first of the next month.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_date_within_date_range
documentation:
Return whether a given date falls within the range of two other dates. This function assumes
start < end.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_in_the_past
documentation:
Test whether a datetime is in the past.

Note that we treat the current time as 'in the past' for the sake of this test. This means
that any given datetime will always return True for either this function or
`is_in_the_future`.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_in_the_future
documentation: Test whether a datetime is in the future.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_future_date
documentation: Test whether a date is in the future.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_within_the_last_year
documentation: Test whether a date is one year or less before today's local date.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_within_the_last_week
documentation: Test whether a date is within a week ago of today's dates inclusive of the earliest date.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.latest_date_for_day
documentation:
Given an integer day of a month, return the latest date with that day of the month,
bounded by the supplied start_date and end_date. If no such date exists, return None.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.next_date_with_day_of_month
documentation:
Given a starting `date`, return the next date with the specified `day_of_month`.

If the day of the month doesn't exist in the next month, return the nearest date that does.

For example:

> next_date_with_day_of_month(date=date(2020, 1, 31), day_of_month=31)
>
> ```
> returns
>
> date(2020, 2, 29).
> ```

```
raises TypeError

if a datetime is used for 

raises ValueError

if the day of month is invalid
```

Note: Python's datetime is a subclass of date, so the type checker will not prevent passing it
to this function.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.date_iterator
documentation: Iterate through dates between two dates

functions.name: xocto.localtime.system_timezone
documentation: Get the current system timezone.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_dst
documentation:
Indicate whether the given time (and timezone is in daylight savings time (DST or not).

Raises:
    ValueError if `local_time` is a naive datetime.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_localtime_midnight
documentation:
Return whether the supplied datetime is at midnight (in the site's local time zone).

Note, the supplied datetime, which should be timezone aware, may be in any timezone,
providing it corresponds to the moment of midnight in the site's local time zone.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.is_aligned_to_midnight
documentation: Return whether this range is aligned to localtime midnight.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.consolidate_into_intervals
documentation:
Given a sequence of dates, return tuples of (inclusive) boundaries of the sub-sequences where
the dates are consecutive.

If a date does not form a continuous interval with any other dates - i.e. it is separated by at
least a day before and after the others - it forms an interval with itself as both boundaries.

For example, if passed:
[
    <Date year=2020, month=1, day=1>,
    <Date year=2020, month=1, day=2>,
    <Date year=2020, month=1, day=4>,
    <Date year=2020, month=1, day=5>,
]
it would return:
[
    (<Date year=2020, month=1, day=1>, <Date year=2020, month=1, day=2>),
    (<Date year=2020, month=1, day=4>, <Date year=2020, month=1, day=5>),
]

functions.name: xocto.localtime.period_exceeds_one_year
documentation:
Returns true if the passed period exceeds one year.

Edge cases such as leap years and daylight savings time are handled, where a simple approach
using only relativedelta would not be sufficient.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.parse_date
documentation: Returns a datetime.date for a given ISO format date string.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.parse_dt
documentation:
Returns a datetime.datetime for a given ISO format date/time string.

The date/time must be naive - not include timezone information.

functions.name: xocto.localtime.strftime
documentation:
Format a tz aware datetime in localtime.

Wrapper for `datetime.strftime`.

functions.name: xocto.urls.pop_url_query_param
documentation:
Pop one query string value off a URL returning both the modified URL and popped value.

E.g.
\>>> pop_url_query_param('<https://localhost/x/y/x?a=1&b=2&c=3>', 'b')
('<https://localhost/x/y/x?a=1&c=3>', '2')

Missing values return an unchanged URL and None as the attribute value.
\>>> pop_url_query_param('<https://localhost/x/y/x?a=1&b=2&c=3>', 'd')
('<https://localhost/x/y/x?a=1&b=2&c=3>', None)

Raises a ValueError if the requested param has multiple values.
\>>> pop_url_query_param('<https://localhost/x/y/x?a=1&a=2>', 'a')
Traceback (most recent call last):

> ...

ValueError: ...

functions.name: xocto.urls.parse_file_destination_from_url
documentation:
Split a destination URL into a pyFileSystem URL, destination path and upload path.

A common problem when uploading files to file-systems like FTP or SFTP is that uploads are not
atomic. That means that when uploading a file for another party to download then is a chance
that they will see a partially uploaded file. To avoid that problem it's common to upload the
file to an intermediary (upload) path and then move it to the final (destination) path. That
approach works because while the upload is not atomic, the server-side rename/move is.

When configuring pyFileSystems it's nice to use a single URL rather than separate values for
HOST, PORT, PATH, USERNAME, PASSWORD, etc. This function extends those ergonomics by allowing a
single URL to specific both the upload and final destination locations.

The "Destination URLs" taken by this function are pyFileSystem URLs describing a directory into
which a file should be written. This URL can have an optional `upload` key in the query string.
If that key exists then is is a path to a directory relative to the main URL. This indicates
where the file should be uploaded before being moved to the final destination

This function parses the given "destination URL" into three parts.
\- A pyFileSystem URL which contains both the destination and upload directories
\- The destination path (Relative to the returned FS URL)
\- The upload path (Relative to the returned FS URL)

pyFileSystem filesystems are sandboxed, which means we can't escape them using `..` or similar.
That means that we have to ensure that the returned URL contains both the destination and
upload paths. That means modifying the URL path to be the common ancestor of the two dirs.

The `upload` dir can be a subdir of the destination\` ...
\>>> parse_file_destination_from_url('<ftp://some_server/dir/destination?upload=temp>')
'<ftp://some_server/dir/destination>', '.', 'temp')

... or a parent dir ...
\>>> parse_file_destination_from_url('<ftp://some_server/dir/destination?upload=>..')
('<ftp://some_server/dir>', 'destination', '.')

... Or a sibling ..
\>>> parse_file_destination_from_url('<ftp://some_server/dir/destination?upload=../temp>')
('<ftp://some_server/dir>', 'destination', 'temp')

... Or an absolute path.
\>>> parse_file_destination_from_url('<ftp://some_server/dir/destination?upload=/temp>')
('<ftp://some_server/>', 'dir/destination', 'temp')

If the upload path is not given then it's assumed that the file should be directly uploaded to
the destination dir.
\>>> parse_file_destination_from_url('<ftp://some_server/dir/destination>')
('<ftp://some_server/dir/destination>', '.', '.')

A destination path can be specified with a `destination` query key. This is useful if you need
to force a certain pyFileSystem URL rather than using the full path to the final `destination`.
(c.f. the above example)
\>>> parse_file_destination_from_url('<ftp://some_server/?destination=dir/destination>')
('<ftp://some_server/>', 'dir/destination', '.')

If both `destination` and `upload` query key are specified then they are both used. This is
useful if you need to force a certain pyFileSystem URL rather than finding the nearest common
ancestor of `destination` and `upload`
\>>> parse_file_destination_from_url('<ftp://some_server/?destination=dir/destination&upload=dir/destination/temp>')
('<ftp://some_server/>', 'dir/destination', 'dir/destination/temp')

functions.name: xocto.urls._fix_url_scheme
documentation:
Restore '://' to a URL where urlunparse(urlparse(url)) has removed it.

Strictly speaking URLs which don't have a username, password or host don't need the "//" after
the `schema:`. Python's urlunparse normalises URLs and "may result in a slightly different, but
equivalent URL". This leads to results like:

- urlunparse(urlparse("schema://")) == "schema:".
- urlunparse(urlparse("schema:///root/subdir")) == "schema:/root/subdir"

Unfortunately not all tools cope well with URLs that don't include the '://'. This function
restores the "://" where a trip through urlunparse has removed it.
Private

Casts the given date/time periods as finite periods.

This is useful when working with potentially infinite ranges that are
known to be finite e.g. due to intersection with a finite range.

Raises:

functions.name: xocto.ranges.iterate_over_months
documentation:
Generate a sequence of finite datetime ranges spanning months during the period.

Ranges span each whole month between the start and end times, inclusive.

ie: given a period of (15/01, 15/03) -> [
    (15/01->01/02),
    (01/02->01/03),
    (01/03->15/03)
]

functions.name: xocto.ranges.date_range_for_midnight_range
documentation:
Returns the date range of a midnight-aligned datetime range.

This can be useful where a range is available at datetime granularity,
but is used in functions that operate at date granularity.

Raises:
    ValueError:
        If the range boundaries are in different timezeones.
        If the range boundaries are not midnight-aligned.

classes.name: xocto.ranges.Range

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.__deepcopy__

Return self.

Ranges are immutable, so there is no need to create a copy.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range._is_inside_left_bound

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range._is_inside_right_bound

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.is_disjoint
documentation: Test whether the two ranges are disjoint.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.intersection
documentation: Return the intersection of the two ranges if it exists, or None if they are disjoint.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.union
documentation:
If two ranges overlap (or are adjacent), return an range covering the two ranges. If the
two ranges are disjoint, return None.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.difference
documentation:
Return a range or rangeset consisting of the bits of this range that do not intersect the
other range (or None if this range is covered by the other range).

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.__and__
documentation: Wrapper for intersection to allow the & operator to be used.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.__or__
documentation: Wrapper for union to allow the | operator to be used.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.Range.__sub__
documentation: Wrapper for difference to allow the - operator to be used.

The basic concept of an range of some sort of comparable item, specified by its endpoints
and boundaries (whether the endpoints are inclusive or exclusive). This class provides some
useful helpers for working with ranges.

Usage:

* Ranges are declared by specifying their endpoints and boundaries. If the boundaries is
  omitted, then they are inclusive-exclusive by default

    >>> Range(0, 2, boundaries=RangeBoundaries.EXCLUSIVE_INCLUSIVE)
    <Range: (0,2]>
    >>> Range(0, 2, boundaries="[]")
    <Range: [0,2]>
    >>> r = Range(0, 2)
    >>> print(f"{r}")
    "[0,2)"

* If an endpoint is set as None, then that means that the range is effectively infinite.
  Infinite ranges must have exclusive bounds for the infinite ends. We provide the continuum
  to the continnum
  helper to get an unbounded range.

    >>> Range(0, None)
    <Range: [0, None)
    >>> Range(0, None, boundaries="[]")  # Invalid
    >>> Range.continuum()  # Helper function
    <Range: (None, None)>

* Ranges can be declared for any comparable type

    >>> int_erval: Range[int] = Range(0, 2)
    >>> date_erval: Range[date] = Range(date(2020, 1, 1), date(2020, 6, 6))
    >>> string_erval: Range[str] = Range("ardvark", "zebra")  # Uses lexical ordering

* Ranges are themselves comparable. Two ranges are ordered by their start, with their end used
  to break ties

    >>> sorted([Range(1, 4), Range(0, 5)])
    [<Range: [0,5)>, <Range: [1,4)>]
    >>> sorted([Range(1, 2), Range(None, 2)])
    [<Range: [None,2)>, <Range: [1,2)>]
    >>> sorted([Range(3, 5), Range(3, 4)])
    [<Range: [3,4)>, <Range: [4,5)>]
    >>> sorted([Range(0, 2, boundaries=b) for b in RangeBoundaries])
    [<Range: [0,2)>, <Range: [0,2]>, <Range: (0,2)>, <Range: (0,2]>]

* The `in` operator is provided to check if an item of the range type is within the
  the range

    >>> 0 in Range(0, 2)
    True
    >>> 2 in Range(0, 2)
    False
    >>> date(2020, 1, 1) in Range(date(2020, 1, 2), date(2020, 1, 5))
    False

* The `intersection` function (which is aliased to the and (&) operator) will return the
  overlap of two ranges, or None if they are disjoint
    >>> Range(0, 2).intersection(Range(1, 4))
    <Range: [1,2)>
    >>> Range(1, 2) & Range(3, 4)
    None

* The `is_disjoint` function will tell you if two ranges are disjoint
    >>> Range(0, 2).is_disjoint(Range(3, 5))
    True
    >>> Range(0, 2).is_disjoint(Range(2, 5))  # Since the default is not right-inclusive
    True

* The `union` function (which is aliased to the or (|) operator), will return a range which
  covers two overlapping (or touching) ranges, or None if they are disjoint.
    >>> Range(0, 2).union(Range(1, 3))
    <Range: [0,3)>
    >>> Range(0, 2) | Range(2, 4)
    <Range: [0,4)>
    >>> Range(0, 2) | Range(3, 4)
    None
    >>> Range(0, 2) | Range(2, 4, boundaries="(]")
    None
    >>> Range(0, 2, boundaries="[]") | Range(3, 4)  # Since Range doesn't understand that 2 and 3 are adjacent.
    None

* The `difference` function (which is aliased to the subtraction (-) operator), will return a
  range which contains the bit of this range which is not covered by the other range, or a
  rangeset which contains the bits of this range which are not covered (or None if the other
  range covers this one).
    >>> Range(0, 4) - Range(2, 4)
    <Range: [0,2)>
    >>> Range(0, 4) - Range(2, 3)
    <RangeSet: {[0,2), [3,4)}>
    >>> Range(0, 4) - Range(0, 5)
    None

classes.name: xocto.ranges.FiniteRange

methods.name: xocto.ranges.FiniteRange.intersection
documentation: Intersections with finite ranges will always be finite.

A FiniteRange represents a range that MUST have finite endpoints (i.e. they cannot be None).

This is mostly to add type checking, as we can specify that functions require a finite range
and then skip checking if the endpoints are None.

classes.name: xocto.ranges.HalfFiniteRange

methods.name: xocto.ranges.HalfFiniteRange.intersection
documentation: Intersections with half finite ranges will always be half finite.

This is also for type-checking, but represents a very common range type in Kraken (possibly
the most common).

Specifically, ranges that MUST have a finite inclusive left endpoint and a (possibly infinite)
exclusive right endpoint.

However LeftFiniteInclusiveRightExclusiveRange is a bit of a mouthful so we're going with
HalfFiniteRange.

classes.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet._condense_range_list
documentation:
Given an iterable of source ranges, return a sorted list of ranges where the ranges have
been condensed into the smalles amount of disjoint ranges that matches the input.

For example:

    * [[0,4), [1, 5), [7,9)] -> [[0,5), [7,9)]

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.discard
documentation:
Discarding a range from a range set is equivalent to "cutting away" all the intersections
of that range with the rangeset.

Remove and return an arbitrary set element.

Raises KeyError if the set is empty.

Note that this doesn't require exact matching - the target item just has to be inside one
of the ranges in the set.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.contains_range
documentation: Check a Range is fully contained within another Range within this RanegSet.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.contains_item
documentation: Check if an item is contained by any Range within this RangeSet.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.is_disjoint
documentation: Check whether this RangeSet is disjoint from the other one.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.__len__
documentation: This isn't particularly useful for consumers, but makes equality work nicely.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.intersection
documentation: Return the intersection of this RangeSet and the other Range or RangeSet.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.__and__
documentation: Wrapper for intersection to allow the & operator to be used.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.__or__
documentation: Wrapper for union to allow the | operator to be used.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.complement
documentation:
Get a rangeset representing the ranges between the ranges in this rangeset and infinite
left and right bounds.

methods.name: xocto.ranges.RangeSet.difference
documentation:
Return a rangeset consisting of the bits of this rangeset that do not intersect the other
rangeset.

A RangeSet represents an ordered set of disjoint ranges. It is constructed from an iterable of
Ranges (which can be omited to create an empty RangeSet):

    >>> RangeSet()  # empty RangeSet
    <RangeSet: {}>
    >>> rs = RangeSet([Range(0, 1), Range(2, 4)])  # Single iterable of ranges
    >>> print(f"{rs}")
    "{[0,1), [2, 4)}"

Overlapping Ranges are condensed when they are added to a set:

    >>> RangeSet([Range(0, 3), Range(2, 4)]) == RangeSet([Range(0, 4)])
    True

classes.name: xocto.ranges.HalfFiniteRangeSet
documentation:
This subclass is useful when dealing with half-finite intervals as we can offer stronger guarantees
than we can get with normal RangeSets - mostly around intersections being half-finite etc.

classes.name: xocto.storage.storage.StreamingBodyIOAdapter

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.StreamingBodyIOAdapter.readinto
documentation:
Read bytes into a pre-allocated, writable buffer-like object

Mutates `buffer` and returns the number of bytes read.

Wrapper to adapt a boto3 S3 object to a standard Python "file-like" object

Boto3 returns S3 files as instances as of botocore.response.StreamingBody. These have a `read`
method but are otherwise not quite "file-like" enough to be drop in equivalents of anything in
the normal Python IO hierarchy.

This class is an subclass of io.RawIOBase which works by wrapping a boto StreamingBody. The
storage module uses it so that it can return normal looking Python file objects and hide
whether the backing store is S3 or not.

documentation: Define the interface for subclasses

documentation: Store a file in S3 given its filename and contents. Contents should be UTF-8 encoded.

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.BaseS3FileStore.store_versioned_file
documentation:
Store a file in S3 given its filename and contents. Contents should be UTF-8 encoded.

The bucket must have versioning enabled.
If the key is not known, `make_key_path` should be called to generate it.

```
raises BucketNotVersioned

if the bucket does not have versioning enabled.
```

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.BaseS3FileStore.make_key_path
documentation:
Return the full file (key) path given a namespace and filepath.

This is normally just `{namespace}/{filepath}` except when `self.use_date_in_key_path` is
true, in which case today's date is inserted in between:

    `{namespace}/2021/07/20/{filepath}`

Use this to determine the key path that would be returned by the `store_file*` functions
for the same namespace and filepath.

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.BaseS3FileStore.get_key_or_store_file
documentation:
Return the full key for `namespace` and `filepath`, writing `contents` if there's nothing
already at that key path.

Warning: this is open to race conditions for certain storage backends (S3) because there's
no way to transactionally create an object at a key. E.g.:

    * Process 1 does get_key_or_store_file
    * Process 1 sees there's no file
    * Before process 1 can create the file at the key
    * Process 2 does get_key_or_store_file
    * Process 2 also sees there's no file
    * Whichever process finishes the writing to the key last wins

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.BaseS3FileStore.fetch_text_file

valid for a limited period of time which is specified with `expires_in` in seconds.
Additional response headers can be provided with `response_headers`, using keys such as
`"ResponseContentType"` or `"ResponseContentDisposition"`.

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.S3FileStore.list_s3_keys_page
documentation:
Lists subset of files in the S3 bucket, optionally limited to only those within a given
namespace. S3 keys ending with a forward slash are excluded, on the assumption that they
are folders, not files.

```
param namespace

Limits keys to only those with this prefix

param next_token

next_token supplied from the last returned result

param max_keys

the number of keys to request from the bucket (<1000)

return

Tuple of generator of key names, and next token for next page
```

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.S3FileStore.list_files

Lists all files in the S3 bucket, optionally limited to only those within a given
namespace. S3 keys ending with a forward slash are excluded, on the assumption that they
are folders, not files.

```
param namespace

Limits keys to only those with this prefix

return

Generator of key names
```

classes.name: xocto.storage.storage.LocalFileStore

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.LocalFileStore.fetch_file_contents_using_s3_select
documentation:
Localdev version of S3FileStore `fetch_file_contents_using_s3_select`. Does not support scan ranges.
Converts the file to a pandas dataframe and then runs the SQL query on it
by creating an in memory DB with the table which a query can be run on. As the file is loaded into memory,
this is not recommended for use with very large files.

While this should return the same results as the S3FileStore version,
there may be small discrepancies in the results being returned.

methods.name: xocto.storage.storage.LocalFileStore.list_files
documentation:
Return a generator that lists all files in the tree below the given directory.

`namespace`:                   the directory whose contents we want to list .

Note the following two ways in which the output of this function *differs* from the unix
`ls` command, in order to mimic the S3 `list` output:

1. It recurses down through subdirectories.
2. It returns the namespace with the result.

So given a directory structure /tmp/my-bucket/a/b/c.pdf, we could expect the following
session:
>>>> store = LocalFileStore('my-bucket')
>>>> store.list_files('a')
['a/b/c.pdf']
For local development.
